summary:
Slave Revolts in the Caribbean (16th–18th centuries) were the persistent armed resistance of enslaved Africans and their descendants against the plantation system, expressing the refusal of millions of people to accept their enslavement and constituting a continuous, often successful challenge to colonial authority throughout the era of the [[event:2341|Atlantic Slave Trade]]. The most common form of resistance was marronage — the escape of enslaved people to form independent communities (maroon communities) in the mountains, forests, and interior regions of Caribbean islands and the American mainland. Maroon communities in Jamaica (the Maroon Wars, 1728–1739 and 1795–1796), Suriname, Brazil (the Quilombo of Palmares, 1605–1694), Cuba, and Saint-Domingue maintained armed independence for decades or centuries, forcing colonial governments to sign formal peace treaties recognizing their freedom.

Larger coordinated revolts occurred throughout the colonial period: Tacky's Revolt in Jamaica (1760) involved thousands of enslaved people; Fedon's Rebellion in Grenada (1795–1796) nearly expelled the British; and the Berbice Slave Uprising (1763) in Suriname briefly established an independent polity under the African-born leader Cuffy (now Guyana's national hero). The culmination of Caribbean slave resistance was the [[event:2335|Haitian Revolution]] (1791–1804), which succeeded where others had failed in overthrowing colonial slavery and establishing an independent Black republic. The persistent and widespread nature of slave resistance — the frequency of revolts, the scale of maroon communities, the constant threat of uprising that shaped plantation management — demonstrates that enslaved people actively contested their condition, refutes any notion of passive acceptance, and reveals the enormous costs (in military force, constant surveillance, and social anxiety) that the slave system imposed even on its beneficiaries.
